commands.author: quengh

target.title: fix(memory): idle-evict cached MemoryIndexManager instances in long-running gateway (v2 with in-flight protection)
target.body:
## Context

This is a revised version of #85910, which was auto-closed by ClawSweeper
within minutes of opening, without any human reviewer input. The bot gave
two reasons:

1. **Claimed duplicate of #40389.** This is incorrect. #40389 (merged
   2026-03-09) fixes *CLI one-shot exit hangs* by calling teardown in CLI
   `finally`. This PR fixes the *complementary long-running daemon path*
   where cached managers need to be evicted during normal daemon runtime,
   not just at process shutdown. The two fixes are non-overlapping and
   both necessary — see "Why this is not a duplicate of #40389" below.

2. **Legitimate technical concern: in-flight protection.**
   > "the proposed default idle sweeper can close a cached memory index
   > manager while an in-flight operation is still using it, so it should
   > not merge as-is. The patch needs active-use protection and config
   > help/docs alignment before it is the safest implementation."

   This PR addresses concern (2) directly — see "In-flight protection"
   section below.

## Problem (same as #85910, retained for reviewers)

In long-running gateway daemons, `MemoryIndexManager` instances are cached
in the module-level `INDEX_CACHE` indefinitely. Each instance owns a
chokidar `FSWatcher`; with `sync.watch=true` (default) recursively watching
the memory directory, this leaks `FSEventWrap` native handles and file
descriptors for every memory/*.md file the watcher has touched.

In a production daemon we measured:

- 11h uptime → 612 `FSEventWrap` native handles accumulated
- RSS grew ~150 MB/h, +1.6 GB over 11h from baseline
- `lsof` showed 488 `memory/*.md` file descriptors held by chokidar's
  `awaitWriteFinish`

Related: #71335.

## Fix

Same approach as #85910 — idle-TTL eviction on `ManagedCache`, wired into
gateway startup as a periodic sidecar:

- `ManagedCache.lastAccessAt` is refreshed on every cache hit and on
  initial set.
- `closeIdleManagedCacheEntries` walks `lastAccessAt`, closes stale
  entries, and isolates `close()` errors so one bad entry does not stop
  the rest.
- `closeIdleMemoryIndexManagers` (in `manager.ts`) layers in the
  embedding-probe-cache cleanup that the manager would otherwise miss.
- `scheduleMemoryIndexIdleEvict` (in `server-startup-post-attach.ts`)
  registers a gateway-lifetime sidecar that calls the idle sweep every
  `idleEvictScanMs` (default 5 min) evicting managers idle for
  `idleEvictMs` (default 15 min). Both intervals are configurable via
  `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sync.idleEvictMs` /
  `idleEvictScanMs`; setting either to `0` disables the sweep.

## In-flight protection (new in v2)

`ManagedCache` now tracks an `inflightCount` map keyed the same way as
`cache` / `lastAccessAt`:

- `acquireManagedCacheKey(cache, key)` increments the count, refreshes
  `lastAccessAt`, and returns an idempotent `release()` function.
- `isManagedCacheKeyBusy(cache, key)` reports whether the count is > 0.
- `closeIdleManagedCacheEntries` now consults `isManagedCacheKeyBusy`
  during both the survey loop and immediately before `close()`. Busy
  entries are skipped, counted, and surfaced via the new `onSkipBusy`
  callback and `skippedBusy` return field.
- `closeManagedCacheEntries` (full teardown) clears `inflightCount`
  alongside `cache` and `lastAccessAt` to keep test runs reproducible.

In `MemoryIndexManager`, a protected `withBusy(fn)` helper wraps the
acquire/release pair in try/finally. Two long-running entry points use
it today:

- `sync(...)` — wraps `runSyncWithReadonlyRecovery` (which covers batch
  reindex, targeted session sync, full sync). The refcount is released
  in the same `.finally()` that nulls `this.syncing`, so they unwind
  together.
- `search(...)` — the public entry point now calls `searchInternal` via
  `withBusy`. This protects the embedding/vector/FTS work plus the
  inline `sync({ reason: "search", force: true })` bootstrap that runs
  on the first call after process start.

`close()` and `closeMemoryIndexManagersForAgent` clear any residual
`inflightCount` entry alongside the cache slot. The `release()` returned
by `acquireManagedCacheKey` is idempotent, so a release that fires after
`close()` is a no-op.

The sidecar log now mentions deferred entries when present:

```
memory index manager idle eviction: evicted 3 (deferred 1 busy) (remaining 5)
```

This means: even if a batch reindex spans an idle window
(e.g. `lastAccessAt` ages past `idleMs` while the batch is running),
the manager will not be evicted until the batch completes and the
release callback fires. On the next scan the refcount-protected refresh
of `lastAccessAt` will have happened in `release()`, so the entry gets a
full idle window before becoming eligible again.

## Validation

Same monkey-patched validation as #85910:

- With the fix: 8h gateway uptime, RSS 2031 MB → 1859 MB (zero growth)
- Without the fix: 11h, RSS +1.6 GB (~150 MB/h)
- Memory fd count returns to 0 between memory_search invocations

The in-flight protection has not been live-soaked in production yet —
the original monkey-patch lacked it, and our production workload's batch
reindexes are short relative to the 15 min idle threshold so we never
hit the race in practice. The protection is unit-tested with:

1. Mock acquire/release during scan: verifies the busy entry is skipped
   (`skippedBusy` increments, `evicted` stays at 0).
2. After release fires (which refreshes `lastAccessAt`), the next scan
   is a no-op; ageing the entry once more and scanning again evicts it
   cleanly.
3. Mixed scan: one busy stale entry + one idle stale entry → exactly the
   idle one is closed.

## Tests

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-cache.test.ts` — +6 new
  tests for `acquireManagedCacheKey` / `isManagedCacheKeyBusy` / busy
  deferral / refcount / lastAccessAt refresh / inflightCount cleanup.
  17 tests pass (was 11).
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.idle-gc.test.ts` — +2 new
  tests for in-flight protection at the manager-level entry point;
  existing `returns zero` test now also asserts `skippedBusy === 0`.
  7 tests pass (was 5).
- All other extension-memory tests (711 total) still pass.
- `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts` (41 tests) still
  passes.
- `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` (27 tests) still passes.
- `pnpm run lint`, `pnpm run tsgo:core`, `pnpm run tsgo:extensions`,
  `pnpm run tsgo:test`, and `pnpm run build` all green.

## Risk / Compatibility

- Default 15 min idle threshold unchanged from #85910 — conservative
  enough to comfortably outlive typical bursts of `memory_search` calls
  within a session.
- Active-use protection is enabled by default (no opt-out flag); safer
  default per ClawSweeper's recommendation. Disabling the sweep
  entirely (`idleEvictMs=0` or `idleEvictScanMs=0`) still works as in
  #85910.
- `INDEX_CACHE` size only shrinks when no in-flight operations
  reference the manager. Worst-case dwell is now `idleMs + scanMs + max
  in-flight op duration` (was `idleMs + scanMs`).
- Behavior for short-lived CLI invocations is unchanged
  (`closeAllMemoryIndexManagers` is still the exit path, and it clears
  `inflightCount` along with the rest of the cache).
- Public `MemoryPluginRuntime.closeIdleMemorySearchManagers` return
  type grows a new field `skippedBusy: number`. Existing callers that
  only read `evicted`/`remaining` continue to work; structural typing
  in TypeScript handles the additive change cleanly. The fallback path
  in `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` returns the new field as well.

## Why this is not a duplicate of #40389

|  | #40389 | This PR |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Process exit (CLI `finally`) | Periodic sidecar on gateway lifetime |
| Frequency | Once per process | Every `idleEvictScanMs` (default 5 min) |
| Scope | Close *all* cached managers | Close idle managers only |
| Protection | N/A (process is exiting) | `isManagedCacheKeyBusy` skip + `withBusy` wrap |
| Surface | CLI one-shot hangs on exit | Daemon FD/RSS growth during normal operation |

They are complementary: #40389 covers process shutdown; this PR covers
the continuous lifecycle of long-running daemons that never reach that
shutdown path until restart.

## Out of scope

- Does NOT change `sync.watch` default value (#71335 will handle that
  separately).
- Does NOT refactor the watcher to be singleton-per-workspace (much
  larger change).
- Does NOT introduce an opt-out for active-use protection — it's a
  safety net that should always be on; the only configurable knob is
  whether the sweep itself runs.

Refs #71335.

target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",
  "hasRawKey": true,
  "exactLookupFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw"
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

Observed result after fix: The current prompt is 512 chars, below the 520 char cap, includes `<omitted_skill_keys>`, preserves the raw advertised key as JSON data, and `formatSkillInfo()` resolves the first advertised key exactly as `skill-00<zero`raw`.

What was not tested: Live model submission was not needed for this prompt-rendering fix; the local proof covers the real prompt builder and CLI exact lookup formatter path.

Local script generated 80 workspace skills with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520`, injected the raw omitted lookup key `skill-00<zero`raw`, and rendered the same prompt path through `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()`. The after case also passes the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup.

Before (`79acf3289d`, pre budgeted lookup-key note):

Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (`db926508c4`):


target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: fix(codex): gate CLI session resume behind plugin approval
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `codex.cli.session.resume` did not consistently pass through plugin approval before node dispatch.
- Solution: require a restricted plugin approval for resume, limited to `allow-once` and `deny`.
- What changed: the resume policy now requests approval before `ctx.invokeNode()`, uses the standard plugin approval timeout instead of the longer node resume timeout, fails closed when approval delivery is unavailable or denied, and the Control UI renders only the decisions allowed by the approval request.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): session listing, node-host resume implementation, and Codex CLI spawn behavior are unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

`codex.cli.session.resume` can resume an existing local Codex CLI session with a caller-supplied prompt. Since this is a dangerous node-host command, the Gateway policy should require an explicit operator decision before forwarding the request to the node.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex CLI session resume now requires plugin approval before node dispatch, and the Control UI shows only the decisions permitted for this approval.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, branch `codex-resume-approval`, head `713c83eba2ce`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx .artifacts/codex-resume-real-behavior-proof.ts`; `node .artifacts/codex-resume-control-ui-proof.mjs`; targeted Vitest and `oxfmt --check` on touched files.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied redacted runtime logs from a live local Gateway + paired node run and from a real Control UI bundle opened in Google Chrome:

```text
[gateway] http server listening (10 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, codex, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice)
[proof ...] approval runtime client connected
[proof ...] operator invoke client connected
[proof ...] starting headless node host codex-proof-node-...
[gateway] device pairing auto-approved device=<node-device-id> role=node
[proof ...] approved node pairing request ... commands=["system.run.prepare","system.run","system.which","browser.proxy","codex.cli.session.resume","codex.cli.sessions.list"]
[proof ...] node connected: nodeId=<node-device-id> commands include codex.cli.session.resume after node-pair approval
[proof ...] non-dangerous list invoke succeeded: sessionCount=1 payloadJSON=present
[proof ...] plugin approval requested: id=plugin:... title="Resume Codex CLI session" severity="critical" toolName="codex.cli.session.resume"
[proof ...] plugin approval resolved with deny: id=plugin:...
[proof ...] deny resume result error: {"name":"GatewayClientRequestError","gatewayCode":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"Codex CLI session resume requires plugin approval.","details":{"approvalId":"plugin:...","decision":"deny","code":"PLUGIN_APPROVAL_REQUIRED"}}
[proof ...] plugin approval requested: id=plugin:... title="Resume Codex CLI session" severity="critical" toolName="codex.cli.session.resume"
[proof ...] plugin approval resolved with allow-once: id=plugin:...
[proof ...] allow-once resume result error: {"name":"GatewayClientRequestError","gatewayCode":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"Error: Missing or invalid Codex CLI session id.","details":{"nodeError":{"code":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"Error: Missing or invalid Codex CLI session id."},"code":"INVALID_REQUEST"}}
[proof ...] proof completed

[control-ui-proof ...] branch=codex-resume-approval head=713c83eba2ce
[control-ui-proof ...] loaded real Control UI bundle from http://127.0.0.1:5173/
[control-ui-proof ...] approval title="Resume Codex CLI session" toolName=codex.cli.session.resume
[control-ui-proof ...] rendered decision buttons=["Allow once","Deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] always allow visible=false
[control-ui-proof ...] click Deny -> handleExecApprovalDecision calls=["deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] rerendered decision buttons=["Allow once","Deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] click Allow once -> handleExecApprovalDecision calls=["allow-once"]
[control-ui-proof ...] proof completed
```

- Observed result after fix: `codex.cli.sessions.list` still dispatches without plugin approval; `codex.cli.session.resume` creates a plugin approval request before node dispatch; denied approval returns `PLUGIN_APPROVAL_REQUIRED` without dispatch; `allow-once` proceeds to the node handler; the resume approval uses the normal plugin approval timeout rather than the long node resume timeout; the Control UI renders only `Allow once` and `Deny`, with no `Always allow` button.
- What was not tested: remote multi-device deployment and a successful live Codex model turn after resume. The allow path intentionally used an invalid session id so the proof could verify dispatch reaches the node handler without running a model turn.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this change, the Codex resume node-invoke policy dispatched to the node without creating a plugin approval request.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the Codex CLI resume node-invoke policy did not request plugin approval before dispatching to the node.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test covering approval behavior for this resume policy, and the Control UI approval prompt did not preserve plugin approval `allowedDecisions`.
- Contributing context: session listing and session resume are registered together, but only resume needs approval gating.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/node-cli-sessions.test.ts`, `src/gateway/node-invoke-plugin-policy.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: resume requires plugin approval, does not dispatch when approval delivery is unavailable or denied, does not pass the long node resume timeout into the approval request, forwards only after `allow-once`, and the Control UI renders only the decisions allowed by the plugin approval request.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is local to the Codex node-invoke policy, plugin approval request propagation, and the Control UI approval prompt.
- Existing test that already covers this: none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex CLI session resume through this node command now requires plugin approval before dispatch. The approval prompt offers `Allow once` or `Deny`; it does not offer `Always allow` for this resume path.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
resume request -> node dispatch

After:
resume request -> plugin approval (allow-once / deny) -> node dispatch only after allow-once
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes — this narrows the resume execution path by requiring explicit plugin approval before node dispatch.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: command dispatch is narrowed by a fail-closed approval gate.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local development checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: Codex extension
- Relevant config: temporary local OpenClaw state/config, redacted in logs

### Steps

1. Run the live Gateway + paired node proof.
2. Run the real Control UI decision-rendering proof.
3. Run targeted regression tests and formatting checks.

### Expected

- Resume dispatch only happens after an `allow-once` plugin approval decision.
- Denied approval and unavailable approval runtime do not dispatch to the node.
- The Control UI shows only `Allow once` and `Deny` for the Codex resume approval.

### Actual

## Real behavior proof

Latest follow-up rebased onto current `origin/main`, resolved refreshed generated config/plugin SDK baseline conflicts, and reran the exact-head behavior proof.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR checkout on host `patrick`, branch `feature/mention-pattern-policy-clean`, head `84aad62da6`, running the real source resolver, Telegram group peer id helper, mention matcher, and inbound mention decision code through Node/tsx against redacted OpenClaw-style config/message facts. This does not exercise live external delivery.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `cd /home/patrick/projects/openclaw-mention-pattern-policy && HEAD_SHA=84aad62da65b8cc431792b4122fc0d7221877a22 HEAD_SHORT=84aad62da6 node --import tsx /tmp/pr70864-proof-02d093.mjs`

```text
{"topic":7,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:7","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":true,"wasMentioned":true,"shouldSkip":false,"skipReason":null}
```

target.title: feat(secrets): add Bitwarden/Vaultwarden secret provider
target.body:
## Summary - **Problem:** OpenClaw has no Bitwarden/Vaultwarden integration for secret management. Users who rely on Bitwarden (55M+ users) cannot store API keys and tokens in their vault. - **Why it matters:** Issues #58 and #24875 request this. Bitwarden is the most popular open-source password manager and is self-hostable via Vaultwarden — a natural fit for OpenClaw's self-hosted audience. - **What changed:** Added `BitwardenSecretProvider` implementing the `SecretProvider` interface from PR #16663, with 34 unit tests. Supports `${bw:item-name/field-name}` syntax. - **What did NOT change:** No changes to existing code. This PR adds two new files only. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Security hardening ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #58 - Closes #24875 - Depends on #16663 (SecretsProvider interface — must land first) - Related #17311 (env/keyring/1Password providers) - Related #7916 (encrypted API keys / secrets management) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes New secret reference syntax for Bitwarden: ```json5 { "secrets": { "providers": { "bw": { "serverUrl": "https://vaultwarden.example.com", "collectionId": "openclaw-secrets" } } }, "models": { "providers": { "anthropic": { "apiKey": "${bw:anthropic-key/password}" } } } } ``` Field extraction supports: `password` (default), `username`, `notes`, `uri`, `totp`, and custom fields by name. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — adds a new secrets provider that reads from Bitwarden CLI - New/changed network calls? `No` — delegates to `bw` CLI which handles all network communication - Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — spawns `bw` CLI via `execFile` (no shell) - Data access scope changed? `No` - Risk + mitigation: - `bw` CLI is invoked via `execFile` (not `exec`) — no shell injection possible - BW_SESSION and other credentials are passed via env to the child process, never written to disk or logged - `--nointeraction` and `--raw` flags prevent interactive prompts and ensure clean output parsing - All errors are mapped to `BitwardenCliError` with actionable hints, never exposing raw vault data ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (tested), Linux/Windows (mocked) - Runtime: Node.js 22+ - Requires: Bitwarden CLI (`bw`) installed and logged in ### Steps 1. Install Bitwarden CLI: `brew install bitwarden-cli` or `npm install -g @bitwarden/cli` 2. Log in: `bw login` then `export BW_SESSION=$(bw unlock --raw)` 3. Create a test item: `bw get template item | jq '.name="test-key" | .login.password="sk-test-123"' | bw encode | bw create item` 4. Configure OpenClaw: add `${bw:test-key/password}` as an API key reference 5. Verify resolution ### Expected - Secret resolves to `sk-test-123` - Cache prevents repeated CLI calls within TTL ### Actual - Works as described (verified via unit tests) ## Evidence - [x] 34 unit tests, all passing - [x] 0 lint errors (oxlint) - [x] 0 format issues (oxfmt) - [x] Tests cover: getSecret (14 tests), setSecret (3), listSecrets (3), testConnection (4), error handling (4), configuration (6) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: All 34 test cases covering field extraction, cache, error mapping, env isolation, auth modes - Edge cases checked: missing fields, null notes/URIs, locked vault, unauthenticated state, multiple item matches, CLI not installed - What I did **not** verify: Live Bitwarden vault (tests use mocked CLI responses). Live verification is possible once PR #16663 lands and the provider can be wired into `buildSecretProviders()`. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` — entirely opt-in, no existing behavior changes - Config/env changes? `Yes` — new `secrets.providers.bw` config section (only used if configured) - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert: Remove `bw` from `secrets.providers` config, or revert to plain-text API keys - Files/config to restore: `openclaw.json` (remove `secrets.providers.bw` section) - Known bad symptoms: `BitwardenCliError` with actionable hint messages for every failure mode ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: PR #16663 may not land or the `SecretProvider` interface may change - Mitigation: This PR depends on #16663 and documents the dependency clearly in code. If the interface changes, this provider will be updated to match. - Risk: `bw` CLI output format may change across versions - Mitigation: Tests mock CLI output based on current documented format. The JSON structure is stable and documented by Bitwarden. ## AI-Assisted - [x] This PR was AI-assisted (Claude) - [x] Fully tested (34 unit tests) - [x] I understand what the code does - [x] Verified against official Bitwarden CLI documentation (https://bitwarden.com/help/cli/) Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

Greptile Summary

Adds a Bitwarden/Vaultwarden secret provider (`BitwardenSecretProvider`) that implements the `SecretProvider` interface from PR #16663, enabling `${bw:item-name/field-name}` secret references. The provider uses `execFile` (no shell) for CLI invocation, isolates credentials via child process env overrides, and includes comprehensive error mapping with actionable hints. - **TOTP caching bug**: TOTP codes (which rotate every ~30 seconds) are cached with the general TTL (default 5 minutes), meaning stale/invalid codes will be returned after the first 30 seconds — causing silent authentication failures. - **`setSecret` notes duplication**: When creating a new item with `field === "notes"`, the value is stored in both the `notes` property and as a custom field named `"notes"` due to an incomplete exclusion in the ternary condition on the `fields` array. - **`setSecret` error swallowing**: The bare `catch` block when checking for existing items treats all errors (vault locked, CLI missing, network errors) as "item not found," masking the real error and attempting an item creation that will fail with a confusing secondary error. - Good security practices: `execFile` over `exec`, env isolation without mutating `process.env`, `--nointeraction` and `--raw` flags. - Thorough test suite with 34 tests, though missing coverage for the notes-on-create and TOTP caching edge cases.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- This PR has logic bugs that should be fixed before merging, but they won't break existing functionality since this is an additive, opt-in feature. - Score of 3 reflects two confirmed logic bugs (TOTP caching returning stale codes, notes field duplication on create) and one error-handling issue (bare catch swallowing CLI errors in setSecret). None of these affect existing code since this is a new, opt-in provider, but they will cause incorrect behavior for users who adopt it. The security model is sound (execFile, env isolation, no shell injection surface). - Pay close attention to `src/config/bitwarden-secret-provider.ts` — specifically the `getSecret` TOTP caching path (line 221-225) and the `setSecret` create-new-item branch (lines 238-269). Last reviewed commit: 35578de (2/5) Greptile learns from your feedback when you react with thumbs up/down!

Key changes: - Replace "Dreaming" with "梦境" for all Dreaming-related strings - Improve jitter/stagger related translations for clarity - Fix untranslated strings in agent tabs, notes, and stats sections - Localize Tailscale serve references - Standardize zh-CN language display names - Update common section values for consistency ## Summary - Problem: Many Control UI strings remained in English or used awkward translations when viewed in zh-CN locale - Solution: Rewrote 30+ translations with clearer, more natural Chinese alternatives; added translations for previously untranslated strings - What changed: Updated `ui/src/i18n/locales/zh-CN.ts` - What did NOT change: All other locale files, non-locale source code, functionality or behavior ## Motivation Chinese-speaking users of the Control UI frequently encountered English strings or confusing translations (e.g. "Dreaming" left untranslated, "抖动" for stagger/jitter which has a different meaning in Chinese). This PR improves the overall zh-CN user experience by replacing ambiguous or overly technical translations with clearer alternatives more natural for native speakers. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR N/A ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Chinese (Simplified) locale had untranslated or awkward strings in the Control UI - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (2026.5.20) with zh-CN locale - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Set Control UI language to 简体中文 and hard-refreshed - **Evidence after fix:** - **Observed result after fix:** All targeted strings now display in improved Chinese (e.g., "梦境" instead of "Dreaming", "定时任务" instead of "Cron Jobs") - **What was not tested:** Visual rendering across all UI panels (spot-checked main sections) ## Root Cause N/A — not a bug fix. ## Regression Test Plan N/A — not a bug fix. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes zh-CN Control UI users will see: - "Dreaming" → "梦境" throughout dreaming section - "Cron Jobs" → "定时任务" in tabs, stats, and form fields - "Tailscale serve" → "Tailscale 服务" - "Browser enabled" → "浏览器已启用" - "Tool profile" → "工具配置" - Improved stagger/jitter terminology for clarity - Previously untranslated strings in restart confirmation dialog, empty job states, and job form now in Chinese ## Security Impact - New permissions/capabilities? **No** - Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No** - New/changed network calls? **No** - Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** - Data access scope changed? **No** ## Human Verification - Verified scenarios: Compared each changed string against the English source (en.ts) to ensure accuracy - Edge cases checked: Template interpolation patterns ({count}, {value}, {time}) preserved correctly; braces balanced; TypeScript structure intact - What I did **not** verify: Visual rendering (locale is compiled by Vite at build time) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? **Yes** - Config/env changes? **No** - Migration needed? **No** ## Risks and Mitigations None — locale data change only, no logic, no API surface, no new dependencies. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Chinese (Simplified) locale had untranslated or awkward strings in the Control UI - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (2026.5.20) with zh-CN locale selected in Control UI settings - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. target.title: fix(imessage): detect and recover anchorless watch payloads before routing
target.body:
## Summary
- Fix iMessage group link-preview watch payloads that lose their conversation anchor (`chat_id=0`, empty `chat_guid` / `chat_identifier`, `is_group=false`) before they can be classified as sender DMs.
- Move anchorless recovery/fail-closed drop ahead of live inbound debounce classification so `coalesceSameSenderDms` cannot merge malformed group payloads under the raw `chat:0` DM bucket.
- Keep the existing recovery path for catchup/direct handling and add monitor-level regression coverage for the live coalescing path.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: iMessage group link-preview watch payloads can arrive without usable conversation anchor fields. Before this fix, live notifications entered the inbound debouncer first, so with `coalesceSameSenderDms=true` multiple anchorless group payloads from the same sender could be merged under `imessage:<account>:dm:chat:0:<sender>` before recovery. The merged turn was then repaired using only the first GUID and could route content from another group under the first recovered group.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node.js v22.22.0, OpenClaw source checkout at `e22876fd1befdbda3183cc33fb5162459791a89e`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
env -C /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-41483 \
  /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-41483/node_modules/.bin/vitest run \
  extensions/imessage/src/monitor.watch-subscribe-retry.test.ts \
  extensions/imessage/src/monitor/conversation-repair.test.ts \
  extensions/imessage/src/monitor.gating.test.ts \
  extensions/imessage/src/monitor/coalesce.test.ts

pnpm -C /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-41483 check:test-types

env -C /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-41483 \
  /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-41483/node_modules/.bin/tsx \
  /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-84503-anchorless-proof.mts

OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 \
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=19046 \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-84503-final-runtime/state \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-84503-final-runtime/state/openclaw.json \
node scripts/run-node.mjs --dev gateway

curl -sS -I http://127.0.0.1:19046/
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests       51 passed (51)
Duration    9.29s
```

```text
$ pnpm -C /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-41483 check:test-types
$ pnpm tsgo:test
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

```text
=== PR #84503 anchorless iMessage proof ===
PASS single anchorless payload recovers to group chat_id=101
PASS unrecoverable anchorless payload is dropped fail-closed
BEFORE fix: raw payloads share imessage:default:dm:chat:0:+15550001111; merged GUIDs=["GUID-GROUP-A","GUID-GROUP-B"] route=chat_id:101
AFTER fix: payloads repair before debounce; keys=imessage:default:chat:101:+15550001111 / imessage:default:chat:202:+15550001111; routes=chat_id:101,chat_id:202
PASS coalescing risk closed: cross-chat anchorless payloads no longer enter one raw DM bucket
Overall: PASS
```

```text
2026-05-24T15:58:29.888+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 9.6s)
2026-05-24T15:58:30.678+08:00 [gateway] ready
```

```text
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
```

**Observed result after fix**: Anchorless live payloads are repaired before entering the inbound debouncer. Recovered group payloads have `is_group=true` and distinct group chat IDs before debounce classification, so `coalesceSameSenderDms` no longer places different malformed groups into the same raw `chat:0` DM bucket. Single anchorless payload recovery and unrecoverable fail-closed drops still work.

**What was not tested**: A real BlueBubbles/iMessage live watch feed was not connected in this Linux environment. The gateway startup used `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, so it proves the patched OpenClaw runtime starts and serves HTTP, but it does not exercise a live macOS Messages database or live BlueBubbles transport. The iMessage behavior proof uses the real monitor/conversation-repair/coalesce TypeScript modules with redacted synthetic message IDs and handles.

Fixes #84470

target.title: fix(agents): match runtime policy entries when session provider is empty
target.body: ## Summary - Session entries persisted with a bare model id (no `/` prefix) silently fell through `agents.defaults.models["/"].agentRuntime` policy lookup. `modelEntryMatchKind` in `src/agents/model-runtime-policy.ts` evaluated `normalizeProviderId("anthropic") !== normalizeProviderId("")` → returned `"none"` for every provider-prefixed entry, so a configured `agentRuntime: claude-cli` was never matched. - Consequence on a production gateway running Claude subscription OAuth via `anthropic:claude-cli`: requests fell through to the embedded Anthropic-Messages harness, which sent the OAuth token (`sk-ant-oat01-…`) to `/v1/messages` and got back 404 `model_not_found`. All anthropic auth profiles cascaded into 40-min cooldown; every subsequent request surfaced `All models failed (3): … No available auth profile for anthropic` until cooldown expired. Concurrent cron/heartbeat runs that constructed a fresh runtime context with the qualified ref kept succeeding through `provider=claude-cli`. - Fix: treat empty/undefined caller provider as "no constraint" — the entry's slash-prefix is itself authoritative. Mirror in `modelKeyIsProviderWildcard`. When the caller provider is empty and multiple distinct providers would match the same bare model id, refuse to silently pick — return `{}` instead of relying on `Object.entries` order. Then short-circuit `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` (`src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`) on known CLI aliases when caller provider is empty, because the `${provider}:${runtime}` Map lookup can't hit with empty provider. Refs `agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` sessions `d7034f5d-ee47-4c1f-95eb-e10c5793c8df` and `b74095ee-b89b-4411-a991-46da76cd84cd`. ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: session entries that persisted bare model ids (no `/` prefix) silently fell through `agentRuntime: claude-cli` policy and routed Slack DMs to the embedded Anthropic-Messages harness, where Claude subscription OAuth tokens 404 against `/v1/messages` and cascade all anthropic auth profiles into cooldown. Real environment tested: Production OpenClaw 2026.5.20 gateway running on Proxmox LXC CT 112 (Debian, Node 24.13.0). Carries 8 active agents (main/Dobby, gabe, gabe-voice, researcher, forge, architect, marcus, scribe), 27 cron jobs, live Slack channel (`agent:main:slack:direct:u01q3fya3s4`), `anthropic:claude-cli` OAuth subscription auth (Claude Pro Max). Bundle-level equivalent of this patch applied to the installed `dist/model-runtime-policy-pAmabiPO.js` + `dist/model-runtime-aliases-CF-sDqQj.js`, gateway restarted, real Slack DM sent. Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Applied the equivalent patch to the two installed bundle files, verified with `node -c`. 2. `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway.service`. 3. Sent a real Slack DM to the `main` agent from Brian's user account through the existing `agent:main:slack:direct:u01q3fya3s4` channel: "Confirm your runtime: which provider+model is serving this reply, and was your auth profile in cooldown?" 4. Tailed `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-24.log` for matching events. 5. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-runtime-policy.test.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts` — 28 tests pass. Evidence after fix: Gateway log lines triggered by the Slack DM (timestamps CDT): ``` 02:30:50.766 cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=claude-opus-4-7[1m] promptChars=834 trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=27c5793ef4a7 reuse=reusable 02:31:04.215 claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-opus-4-7[1m] durationMs=13431 rawLines=44 ``` Dobby's self-reported reply over Slack: > "Runtime: claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7[1m] via anthropic:claude-cli (OAuth, Claude Pro Max). Not in cooldown — auth healthy, fallbacks armed (opus-4-7, opus-4-6). Gateway came up 9m ago on 2026.5.20 (e510042)." Test suite outputs (paired with the real-environment evidence above, not standalone): - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-runtime-policy.test.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts` — `Test Files 3 passed (3), Tests 28 passed (28)`. - `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs ` — `exit=0`. - `pnpm tsgo:core` — `exit=0`. - `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck core green; pre-existing `src/plugins/document-extractors.runtime.test.ts(53,74): error TS2554` on clean `origin/main e9ca3115f0` and unrelated to this patch (verified by stash/un-stash). Observed result after fix: same Slack DM lane that was 404-cascading and cooldown-spinning yesterday now routes through the `claude-cli` subprocess. `provider=claude-cli`, `durationMs=13.4s`, 44 raw output lines, no `embedded run` events, no `errorPreview`, no `auth_profile_failure_state_updated`, no `lane task error`. Concurrent cron jobs continue to succeed in parallel as they did before. What was not tested: `google-gemini-cli` equivalent path — no failing session existed to replay, and the fix is structurally symmetric with `claude-cli` so the same defensive logic applies. Doctor's `codex-route-warnings.ts` callers — they always pass a qualified ref via `parseModelRef`, never empty, so the empty-provider arm of the patch never fires for them. 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: fix(secrets): generate secretref reference docs from the credential matrix
target.body:
## Summary

Generates the SecretRef credential reference docs (`docs/reference/secretref-user-supplied-credentials-matrix.json` and the marker-block lists in `docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md`) from the same registry-backed source that already powers `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts`, so contributor branches stop hitting CI drift on artifacts that should never need hand-editing.

Closes #44289.

- **Single source of truth**: both reference artifacts are now derived from `buildSecretRefCredentialMatrix()`. Updating `src/secrets/target-registry-data.ts` (or any bundled-channel unsupported pattern) no longer requires editing two separate docs files.
- **Generator + check pair**: `scripts/generate-secretref-credential-matrix.ts` now accepts `--check`. Without flags it rewrites the JSON matrix and the supported/unsupported marker blocks in the Markdown reference; with `--check` it exits non-zero with a regenerate hint when either artifact is out of sync.
- **package.json scripts**: `pnpm gen:secretref-docs` (writer) and `pnpm check:secretref-docs` (verifier).
- **Preflight wiring**: `scripts/check.mjs` runs `check:secretref-docs` in the preflight stage so contributor branches catch drift before typecheck/lint, not at the tail of CI.
- **Drift test stays in sync with the writer**: `target-registry.docs.test.ts` now compares on-disk artifacts against the same formatter the script uses, so the test cannot diverge from the writer. The existing registry-shape parity check is preserved as a third independent guard.
- **Formatter unit tests** (`credential-matrix-docs.test.ts`): 10 isolated tests cover ordering, sibling_ref rendering (`channels.googlechat.serviceAccount` compatibility exception), the `auth-profiles.json` subsection annotation, empty-list handling, idempotence, and the missing-marker error paths.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Test

## Scope

- [x] Docs / generated reference
- [x] Tooling / build scripts

## Linked Issue

- Closes #44289

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: The two SecretRef reference artifacts were hand-maintained while only `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts` caught divergence. Editing `src/secrets/target-registry-data.ts` (or adding a bundled-channel unsupported pattern) silently produced reference files that did not match the registry until a later CI failure on an unrelated branch. ClawSweeper triaged this in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/44289#issuecomment-4523949220 and labelled it `clawsweeper:queueable-fix`.

**Real environment tested**: Local macOS 15.7.4 (Darwin 25.4.0 arm64) host running Node v22.22.0 and Corepack-managed pnpm against the branch HEAD `9b8cc969`. No remote services were involved; the change is purely doc generation, package scripts, preflight wiring, and tests. The new generator and check script were run end-to-end against the real working tree, including a destructive drift simulation and a byte-for-byte restore via `gen:secretref-docs`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Five real terminal sessions were run end-to-end against the patched working tree. (1) `pnpm gen:secretref-docs` to confirm idempotence on a clean tree. (2) `pnpm check:secretref-docs` to confirm exit 0 on the generator output. (3) Destructive drift simulation: `cp docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md /tmp/orig.md && sed -i '' '/channels.discord.token/d' docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md && pnpm check:secretref-docs && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts` to verify the check fails closed and the drift test surfaces the same divergence. (4) Regeneration restore: `pnpm gen:secretref-docs && pnpm check:secretref-docs && diff /tmp/orig.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md` to verify byte-for-byte recovery. (5) Full repo guards on the touched paths: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json && node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.json && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/secrets/credential-matrix-docs.test.ts src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts src/secrets/target-registry.test.ts && pnpm format:check <touched files> && pnpm lint <touched files>`.

**Evidence after fix**: Redacted real terminal output from the local working tree, captured at branch HEAD before push.

Generator idempotence on a clean tree:

```
$ pnpm gen:secretref-docs
> OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR=extensions ... tsx scripts/generate-secretref-credential-matrix.ts
Wrote docs/reference/secretref-user-supplied-credentials-matrix.json
docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md already in sync
$ echo $?
0
```

Check mode passes on the generated tree:

```
$ pnpm check:secretref-docs
> OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR=extensions ... tsx scripts/generate-secretref-credential-matrix.ts --check
secretref reference docs are in sync.
$ echo $?
0
```

Destructive drift simulation: removing one supported entry from the docs causes both the check script and the drift test to fail closed:

```
$ sed -i '' '/channels.discord.token/d' docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md
$ pnpm check:secretref-docs
error: docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md marker blocks are out of sync.
Run `pnpm gen:secretref-docs` to regenerate.
[ELIFECYCLE] Command failed with exit code 1.
$ echo $?
1
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts
 FAIL  src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts > credential-surface marker blocks match generator output
 FAIL  src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts > matrix and credential-surface marker blocks describe the same registry shape
Test Files  1 failed (1)
     Tests  2 failed | 1 passed (3)
```

Regeneration restores the tree byte-for-byte:

```
$ pnpm gen:secretref-docs
Wrote docs/reference/secretref-user-supplied-credentials-matrix.json
Updated marker blocks in docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md
$ pnpm check:secretref-docs
secretref reference docs are in sync.
$ diff /tmp/orig.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md
$ echo $?
0
```

Full guards on the touched paths after fix:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
    src/secrets/credential-matrix-docs.test.ts \
    src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts \
    src/secrets/target-registry.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7
 PASS  src/secrets/credential-matrix-docs.test.ts (10 tests) 19ms
 PASS  src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts (3 tests) 649ms
 PASS  src/secrets/target-registry.test.ts (5 tests) 174ms
Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  18 passed (18)

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.json
$ echo $?
0

$ pnpm lint <touched files>
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

$ pnpm format:check <touched files>
All matched files use the correct format.
```

**Observed result after fix**: A contributor editing `src/secrets/target-registry-data.ts` runs `pnpm gen:secretref-docs` once and both reference artifacts update in lockstep. The new preflight `check:secretref-docs` stage of `scripts/check.mjs` surfaces drift in the preflight guards alongside `dup:check:coverage`, before the typecheck or lint stages, so contributor branches no longer hit late CI drift failures on the SecretRef reference docs. The destructive simulation captured above confirms the check fails closed (exit 1) and the regenerator restores the working tree byte-for-byte (`diff` exit 0).

**What was not tested**: This patch does not touch runtime SecretRef resolution or audit behaviour, so the existing live SecretRef resolution coverage in `target-registry.test.ts` is unchanged. No CI workflow changes were exercised end-to-end beyond the preflight wiring already covered by `scripts/check.mjs` in local runs; the actual CI run on this PR exercises the new preflight stage in the real GitHub Actions environment.

## Root Cause

- `scripts/generate-secretref-credential-matrix.ts` only wrote the JSON matrix; the Markdown reference's two marker blocks were edited by hand even though their content is fully determined by the registry.
- `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts` detected drift but provided no path to regenerate, so contributors had to mirror the JSON change into the Markdown by hand and hope the test agreed.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test (new formatter tests in `credential-matrix-docs.test.ts`)
  - [x] Integration / drift test (rewritten `target-registry.docs.test.ts` now mirrors the writer)
  - [x] CI preflight (new `check:secretref-docs` runs alongside `dup:check:coverage`)
- **Target test files**:
  - `src/secrets/credential-matrix-docs.test.ts` (new — formatter contract)
  - `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts` (rewritten — generator parity)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- No runtime behavior change. SecretRef eligibility, resolution, and audit coverage are unchanged.
- `docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md` supported-list entries are now deterministically ordered by id within the `openclaw.json` subsection (instead of registry-walk order). Marker comments, surrounding prose, and the `auth-profiles.json` subsection heading are unchanged. The unsupported list preserves the registry-walk order (`CORE_UNSUPPORTED_SECRETREF_SURFACE_PATTERNS` followed by bundled-channel patterns), matching the existing `getUnsupportedSecretRefSurfacePatterns()` contract.

## Scope boundary

- Only the generator, two reference docs, the drift test, two new tooling scripts, the preflight wiring, the knip allowlist entry, and the CHANGELOG `Unreleased` entry are touched.
- No changes to runtime SecretRef resolution, no new runtime dependencies, no install or CI workflow changes beyond the preflight inclusion.

target.title: fix(ui): scope chat session picker to active agent
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Control UI chat session picker can show sessions from other configured agents because picker search/load-more calls are not scoped by active agent and the popover renders raw `sessions.list` rows.
- Why it matters: multi-agent users can see and accidentally switch into unrelated agent/service sessions from the chat picker.
- What changed: picker requests now include the active `agentId`, and the popover renders rows through the same filtered session-option projection used by the grouped selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): the full Sessions page and gateway `sessions.list` behavior remain unchanged.

AI-assisted: Yes. I understand the code path and verified the targeted behavior locally.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #85963
- Supersedes #85964 after rebasing onto current `main`
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: chat session picker pagination/search built `sessions.list` params without the active `agentId`, then rendered `result.sessions` directly instead of using the existing filtered session option groups.
- Missing detection / guardrail: picker tests covered search/pagination mechanics but not cross-agent/cron/subagent filtering in the popover.
- Contributing context (if known): the grouped selector already had active-agent filtering, but the newer popover path bypassed it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the picker result includes another agent, cron session, and spawned subagent, only active-agent chat sessions render in the popover.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the Control UI projection/render path, so a focused DOM render test covers the visible regression without a browser E2E fixture.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): grouped selector filtering tests in `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` covered the helper but not this popover path.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The chat session picker now shows active-agent chat sessions by default instead of leaking unrelated agent/service rows into the popover.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open chat picker] -> [sessions.list without agentId] -> [raw rows rendered] -> [cross-agent rows visible]

After:
[open chat picker] -> [sessions.list with active agentId] -> [filtered option rows rendered] -> [active-agent sessions visible]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: UI session-list access is narrowed for this picker by passing `agentId` and filtering display rows; broader session management surfaces are unchanged.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm workspace and installed local OpenClaw Control UI
- Model/provider: N/A for test execution
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat
- Relevant config (redacted): multiple configured agents with agent-scoped session keys

### Steps

1. Open the Control UI chat view with multiple configured agents and sessions.
2. Open the chat session picker.
3. Search/load more from the picker.

### Expected

- Picker requests are scoped to the active agent.
- Popover renders only sessions allowed by the grouped session selector filtering.

### Actual

- Before this PR, picker requests were unscoped and the popover rendered raw `sessions.list` rows.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Redacted runtime diagnostic output
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.10s

pnpm check:test-types
completed successfully

pnpm build
completed successfully
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: the Control UI chat session picker can leak unrelated configured-agent/service rows when it requests sessions without the active agent scope and renders raw response rows.
- Real environment tested: local macOS OpenClaw app, Control UI served from the installed app on `127.0.0.1:18789`, with multiple configured agents present. Private session labels, keys, and structure are intentionally redacted from the proof below.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: refreshed the installed Control UI cache, inspected the served HTML/JS/SW, and queried the live gateway with an explicit active-agent filter. No screenshots are attached because the visible picker contains private session names.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
$ curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/ | rg -n 'index-BtIuF4zW\.js\?v=local-session-picker-fix|local-session-picker-fix'
198:    <script type="module" crossorigin src="./assets/index-BtIuF4zW.js?v=local-session-picker-fix"></script>

$ curl -sS 'http://127.0.0.1:18789/assets/index-BtIuF4zW.js?v=local-session-picker-fix' \
  | rg -o 'agentId:EW\(e,e\.sessionKey\)|i=AW\(e,e\.sessionKey,r\)\.flatMap|local-session-picker-fix'
agentId:EW(e,e.sessionKey)
i=AW(e,e.sessionKey,r).flatMap
local-session-picker-fix

$ curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/sw.js \
  | rg -n 'local-session-picker-fix|cacheName: CACHE_NAME|caches\.match'
5:const EMBEDDED_CACHE_VERSION = "2026.5.22-a374c3a5bfd5-local-session-picker-fix";
64:      caches.match(event.request, { cacheName: CACHE_NAME }).then(
86:        .catch(() => caches.match(event.request)),

$ openclaw gateway call sessions.list --json --params '{"agentId":"main","limit":50}' \
  | jq '{agentScopedCount:(.sessions|length), containsForeignAgents:([.sessions[] | select((.agentId // "main") != "main")] | length > 0), visibleRowsRedacted:[.sessions[:5][] | {key:"<redacted>", label:"<redacted>", agentId:(.agentId // "main"), kind:(.kind // null)}]}'
{
  "agentScopedCount": 22,
  "containsForeignAgents": false,
  "visibleRowsRedacted": [
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "group" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the served picker bundle now sends the active `agentId`, projects picker rows through the existing filtered option groups, and the live gateway diagnostic for the active agent reports `containsForeignAgents: false` with session keys/labels redacted.
- What was not tested: public screenshot/recording of the picker, because that would reveal private session names and structure. The runtime diagnostic above is the privacy-preserving proof artifact.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: picker search params include `agentId`; popover filters out other-agent, cron, and spawned subagent rows; targeted UI tests pass; test typecheck passes; full build passes; installed local dist contains the scoped picker request and filtered-row projection.
- Edge cases checked: active session remains visible through existing `resolveSessionOptionGroups` behavior; pagination/search test expectations were updated for the scoped request.
- What you did **not** verify: public browser screenshots in a running Control UI instance, because they would expose private session names and structure.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the picker count can reflect the filtered visible rows while `totalCount` still comes from the server response.
  - Mitigation: server request is now scoped by `agentId`, and existing filtered option logic is reused for display consistency.
